input.background: A major hotel chain conducted a study to develop programs for business travelers staying at convention hotels. The study focused on executives who play golf and revealed that 55% of the executives admitted to cheating at golf. Additionally, 20% of the executives admitted to both cheating at golf and lying in business.
input.question: Given an executive who had cheated at golf, what is the probability that the executive also had lied in business?

output.answer:
Let's define events $A$ and $B$ as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& A=\{\text { Executive who had cheated at golf }\} \\
& B=\{\text { Executive who had lied in business }\}
\end{aligned}
$$

From the study, we know that $55 \%$ of executives had cheated at golf, so $P(A)=.55$. Now, executives who both cheat at golf (event $A$ ) and lie in business (event $B$ ) represent the compound event $A \cap B$. From the study, $P(A \cap B)=.20$. We want to know the probability that an executive lied in business (event $B$ ), given that he or she cheated at golf (event $A$ )-that is, we want to know the conditional probability $P(A \mid B)$. Applying the preceding conditional probability formula, we have

$$
P(B \mid A)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}=\frac{.20}{.55}=.364
$$

Thus, given an executive who had cheated at golf, the probability that the executive also had lied in business is .364.

meta_info.caption: The Conditional Probability Formula- Executives Who Cheat at Golf
